Whether any irregular rhythm occurred in 0–12h. Presence of AF/irregularity can matter for perfusion, but in the immediate post-op period a single episode is common and not strongly specific for later creatinine rise.
Whether any irregular rhythm occurred in 13–24h. A max is a coarse indicator (doesn’t capture duration/severity), giving only weak-to-moderate indirect signal for renal outcomes.

Represents presence of any irregular cardiac rhythm (often AF) in the 25–36h window, which can reduce cardiac output and renal perfusion. Moderately predictive via hemodynamic/illness-severity linkage, but indirect and not kidney-specific.

Captures whether irregular rhythm was absent/present at the lowest point early post-op, when transient arrhythmias are common. Early timing and indirect renal linkage make it a weak predictor of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Minimum irregularity in the 13–24h trajectory phase; persistent absence/presence is only indirectly related to AKI through perfusion and overall instability. The 'min' aggregation is less informative than an 'any/maximum' for capturing arrhythmia burden.

Minimum irregularity in the closest window to outcome, but 'min' tends to reflect periods of regular rhythm and can miss intermittent AF burden. Renal relevance remains indirect, so overall usefulness is limited.

Number/frequency of documenting irregular rhythm in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity/workflow proxy. It does not directly encode renal physiology and is prone to clinician-behavior confounding.
Measurement frequency of irregular rhythm in 13–24h mainly reflects monitoring intensity or concern rather than a renal mechanism. At best it is a nonspecific acuity proxy, so low importance.
Measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing instability prompting closer monitoring, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still largely a care-process variable, so capped low.

Mean time in an 'other' rhythm category early post-op is hard to interpret clinically and likely heterogeneous/noisy. It is not kidney-specific and early postoperative rhythm variability is often transient.

Indicates average presence of non-sinus/non-irregular rhythms in 13–24h, a heterogeneous label with unclear physiologic meaning. Renal linkage is indirect and likely dominated by noise/encoding artifacts.

Average burden of 'other' rhythms in 25–36h could reflect persistent conduction/arrhythmia issues and hemodynamic compromise near the outcome window. Still nonspecific and heterogeneous, so only weak-to-moderate utility.
Any occurrence of an 'other' rhythm in the immediate 0–12h period is common and often transient/artefactual. The category is nonspecific and only indirectly related to renal outcomes.
Any 'other' rhythm in 13–24h may signal ongoing cardiac instability that can impair renal perfusion. However, the label is broad and not kidney-specific, limiting predictive strength.
Any 'other' rhythm in 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and may indicate persistent instability. Still an indirect, heterogeneous rhythm label, so modest importance.
Minimum of 'other' rhythm early post-op is typically uninformative (often just reflects time spent not in that category). It has minimal renal specificity and poor aggregation relevance.
Minimum 'other' rhythm in 13–24h largely captures periods without that rhythm and adds little beyond other rhythm indicators. Indirect renal linkage and redundancy keep importance low.
Minimum 'other' rhythm in 25–36h is a weak summary that can miss intermittent clinically relevant episodes and is largely redundant. Renal relevance remains indirect, so low importance.

Count/frequency of documenting an 'other' cardiac rhythm/regularity in the first 12h is mainly a monitoring/documentation-intensity signal. It is nonspecific for renal perfusion or AKI pathways and heavily proxy-prone.

Measurement frequency of an 'other' rhythm label during 13–24h reflects clinician attention/arrhythmia workup more than kidney physiology. Any renal link is indirect via overall instability, so predictive value for creatinine ratio is low.

Later (25–36h) rhythm documentation frequency may weakly proxy ongoing hemodynamic instability closer to the 60h creatinine outcome. Still primarily a process/monitoring variable rather than a renal-specific mechanism.

Mean heart rate from pulse-ox monitoring in the immediate postop window is a nonspecific marker of stress, pain, anemia, or early shock. Early means can wash out brief hypotensive/low-output episodes and are not kidney-specific.

Mean HR in 13–24h may reflect persistent sympathetic drive/low cardiac output that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion, but remains indirect. Mean aggregation reduces sensitivity to short high-risk episodes.

Sustained tachycardia in the 25–36h window can be a closer-in-time indicator of ongoing low output, sepsis, or bleeding that may precede AKI. Still indirect and not a renal biomarker, so moderate importance.

Peak HR in the first 12h captures acute stress/arrhythmia burden but is common and often transient after cardiac surgery. Renal relevance is indirect via hemodynamic compromise, so limited predictive value.

A high HR maximum in 13–24h may better indicate persistent instability (e.g., AF with RVR, low output) that can reduce renal perfusion. Extremes are more informative than means, but still nonspecific for kidney injury.

Peak HR closer to the outcome window can signal ongoing shock/arrhythmia with potential renal hypoperfusion. Useful as a near-term physiologic stress marker, but remains an indirect proxy rather than kidney-specific.
Minimum HR early postop may reflect sedation, pacing, or transient bradyarrhythmia rather than sustained low cardiac output. Renal linkage is weak and confounded by routine postoperative management.
Low HR minimum in 13–24h could indicate bradyarrhythmia/pacing dependence with possible low output, but is often treatment-related. Overall renal specificity is low and signal is inconsistent.
Later bradycardia minima may occasionally reflect clinically significant conduction issues affecting perfusion, but are frequently iatrogenic or paced. Indirect renal relevance keeps importance low.
Frequency of HR measurements from pulse-ox in 0–12h is primarily monitoring intensity in a routinely monitored period. It does not encode a renal mechanism and is highly proxy-prone.
Measurement count mainly reflects nursing/monitoring practices and patient location/acuity rather than kidney injury. Any association with creatinine ratio would be indirect and weak.
More frequent HR monitoring at 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing instability closer to the 60h creatinine outcome. Still a process variable, so capped at low importance.

Mean apical HR in the first 12h is a nonspecific physiologic marker and may be influenced by sedation, pacing, and postoperative catecholamines. Early timing and mean aggregation limit its renal predictive specificity.

Mean apical heart rate in the 13–24h trajectory phase is a nonspecific marker of stress/pain/arrhythmia and overall hemodynamics. It has only indirect renal relevance (perfusion proxy) and the mean can wash out brief tachy/brady episodes tied to AKI risk.

Mean apical heart rate in the 25–36h leading window may reflect sustained sympathetic drive or low-output states. Still indirect for kidney injury and mean aggregation is less sensitive than extremes for capturing perfusion-threatening events.

Indicator of extreme early postoperative tachycardia (apical source), often driven by pain, catecholamines, or transient instability after bypass. Early window derangements are common and tachycardia is an indirect proxy for renal hypoperfusion rather than a renal-specific signal.

Indicator of peak tachycardia in 13–24h, which can correlate with ongoing shock, arrhythmia, or high vasopressor needs. It is still nonspecific and mainly a general acuity/hemodynamic proxy with modest linkage to later creatinine rise.

Peak tachycardia in the 25–36h window can signal persistent low cardiac output, sepsis, or high catecholamine exposure closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. Extremes are more informative than means and timing is favorable, but renal specificity remains limited.

Indicator of early postoperative bradycardia (apical source), which may reflect conduction issues, pacing, or sedation effects. Renal relevance is indirect and the immediate post-op period is noisy with transient events.

Indicator of minimum heart rate in 13–24h, potentially capturing bradyarrhythmia or excessive beta-blockade/pacing dependence. This is an indirect perfusion proxy with modest prognostic proximity to AKI.

Minimum heart rate in 25–36h may capture clinically meaningful bradycardia/low-output physiology closer to the creatinine outcome. Extremes in the leading window can matter for renal perfusion, though it remains nonspecific and treatment-confounded.
Count/frequency of apical HR measurements in 0–12h primarily reflects monitoring intensity and nursing workflow rather than physiology. Strong proxy penalty and limited direct renal linkage.
Apical HR measurement frequency in 13–24h is mainly a care-process/acuity marker (more checks when unstable). It is not a renal mechanism and is highly confounded by clinician behavior.
Apical HR measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing instability closer to the outcome window. Still predominantly a monitoring-intensity proxy, so it should not score high.

Mean peripheral heart rate in the immediate post-op window is a nonspecific marker of stress and hemodynamics. Early means are noisy and indirect for renal injury, with limited ability to capture brief perfusion-threatening episodes.

Peripheral HR mean in 13–24h reflects overall physiologic stress and hemodynamic state but is not kidney-specific. Mean aggregation reduces sensitivity to short hypotension/arrhythmia periods that more strongly drive AKI.

Peripheral HR mean in 25–36h is closer in time and may reflect sustained shock or catecholamine exposure. However it remains an indirect proxy and less informative than extreme HR measures for renal perfusion risk.
Indicator of peak peripheral tachycardia in 0–12h, often influenced by immediate post-bypass physiology, pain, and pressors. It is an indirect and transient signal with limited renal specificity.

Indicator of peak peripheral tachycardia in 13–24h, which can accompany ongoing instability or arrhythmia. Still a general severity/hemodynamic proxy with only modest predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Binary indicator that heart rate was sourced from a peripheral monitor; this is primarily a monitoring/location artifact. It has minimal renal specificity and is mostly a care-process proxy even though it is in the closest time window.
Peripheral HR source flag in the immediate postop period reflects monitoring setup rather than physiology. Very low renal specificity and high proxy penalty.
Peripheral HR source indicator during 13–24h mainly captures documentation/monitoring context. Weak and indirect relationship to AKI risk compared with true hemodynamic or renal biomarkers.
Peripheral HR source flag near 36h is still a monitoring artifact rather than a renal mechanism. Despite temporal proximity, it is unlikely to add meaningful signal for creatinine rise.

Count/frequency of peripheral HR-source measurements is a monitoring-intensity variable. Per rules, _measured features are mostly clinician-behavior proxies and have little renal specificity, especially early postop.

Measurement frequency of peripheral HR source reflects how/where the patient is monitored rather than renal perfusion or injury. At best a weak proxy for acuity, so low usefulness for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Peripheral HR-source measurement frequency in the 25–36h window remains a documentation/monitoring proxy. Even close to the outcome, it lacks mechanistic linkage to evolving AKI and should not drive a sparse renal model.

Proportion/mean use of mechanical ventilation in 0–12h reflects immediate postop respiratory support and overall illness severity. It is only indirectly related to AKI (via severity/hemodynamics) and early transient ventilation is common.

Sustained mechanical ventilation into 13–24h suggests slower recovery and higher acuity, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still indirect with substantial proxy penalty and mean aggregation may dilute short critical events.

Ongoing mechanical ventilation at 25–36h is a stronger marker of persistent critical illness and potential ongoing shock/inflammation that can precede AKI. It remains indirect (not kidney-specific) but temporal proximity increases prognostic value.

Any mechanical ventilation in the first 12h is expected after cardiac surgery and mainly reflects routine care. Limited renal specificity and early timing reduce its value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Any mechanical ventilation during 13–24h indicates continued respiratory support and higher acuity, which can associate with AKI. However it is a coarse binary proxy and not a direct renal pathway marker.

Any mechanical ventilation in 25–36h is a closer-in-time indicator of persistent critical illness and possible ongoing hemodynamic compromise relevant to AKI evolution. Still a proxy variable, but more informative than earlier epochs.

Minimum mechanical ventilation status over 0–12h is hard to interpret clinically and often just indicates there was some period off the ventilator. Poor aggregation for capturing renal-relevant insults and largely redundant with max/mean.

Minimum ventilation status in 13–24h mainly captures whether the patient was ever not mechanically ventilated, which is a weak and noisy proxy. Low renal specificity and inferior aggregation compared with mean/max.

Minimum ventilation status in 25–36h is a coarse indicator that may reflect brief extubation trials rather than sustained physiology. It is less informative than mean/max for severity and has minimal direct renal linkage.

Frequency of documenting mechanical ventilation in the immediate postop window is mainly monitoring/intensity of care rather than a renal mechanism. Any association with AKI is indirect via overall severity and is heavily proxy-penalized.

Measurement count for being on mechanical ventilation reflects documentation/acuity more than kidney perfusion or injury. Mid-epoch timing adds little specific signal for Hour-60 creatinine beyond general illness severity.

Ongoing frequent documentation of mechanical ventilation late (25–36h) can proxy persistent critical illness and hemodynamic/respiratory instability that may contribute to AKI. Still primarily a monitoring-intensity variable, so capped low.

Mean time on no oxygen device early after surgery suggests rapid extubation/low respiratory support, which loosely correlates with lower overall stress and AKI risk. Renal linkage is indirect and early transient physiology is common.

More time on room air/no device in 13–24h indicates recovery trajectory and less severe illness, which can modestly relate to lower AKI risk. It remains nonspecific and not a direct renal pathway marker.

Sustained time on no oxygen device in the 25–36h window suggests clinical stability close to the outcome and may inversely track risk of renal hypoperfusion/ongoing shock. Still an indirect proxy rather than kidney-specific physiology.

Any occurrence of being on no oxygen device in the first 12h may indicate early stability/extubation, but this period is confounded by routine postop transitions. Renal relevance is indirect and binary max is coarse.

Having any room-air/no-device time in 13–24h suggests some recovery and lower severity, modestly associated with less AKI. As a max/binary indicator it is nonspecific and redundant with mean duration.

Any room-air/no-device time in 25–36h is a near-term marker of stability that can correlate with lower risk of worsening renal function by Hour 60. Still indirect and less informative than sustained exposure measures.

A minimum indicator for no oxygen device in 0–12h is hard to interpret clinically and is largely redundant with other summaries. It provides little kidney-specific information and is dominated by care-process timing.
Minimum/binary representation of no-device status in 13–24h is an awkward aggregation that adds little beyond other oxygen-support features. Renal linkage is indirect and weak.

Minimum/binary no-device status in 25–36h is poorly aligned with capturing clinically meaningful respiratory support burden and is redundant with mean/max. It remains a nonspecific proxy for stability rather than renal physiology.
Measurement frequency of documenting no oxygen device is primarily a charting/monitoring artifact. It has minimal direct relevance to renal injury or perfusion and is strongly proxy-penalized.

Counts of recorded no-device status reflect documentation patterns more than physiology. Any association with creatinine rise is indirect via overall acuity and thus low value for sparse renal prediction.
Even late in the course, how often no-device status is recorded is mainly monitoring intensity rather than a mechanistic AKI signal. Not appropriate for high importance under the _measured rule.

Mean low-flow oxygen use early postop reflects mild-moderate respiratory support needs and overall illness severity, which can weakly correlate with AKI risk. Renal specificity is low and early window abnormalities are often transient.

Represents typical use of low-flow supplemental oxygen in the 13–24h window, a nonspecific marker of respiratory status and overall recovery. Only indirect renal relevance via general illness severity and oxygen delivery; mean use is also a care-pattern proxy.

Average low-flow oxygen use closer to the outcome window may reflect persistent cardiopulmonary limitation. Still indirect for AKI/creatinine rise and largely reflects clinician oxygen titration rather than a renal mechanism.

Binary indicator of any/peak low-flow oxygen use immediately post-op, when transient oxygen needs are common after anesthesia and extubation. Minimal renal specificity and high proxy/standard-care component.

Any/peak low-flow oxygen use in 13–24h may indicate incomplete cardiopulmonary recovery. Weak, indirect association with renal outcomes through overall severity; binary max adds limited physiologic granularity.
Any/peak low-flow oxygen use in 25–36h could signal ongoing hypoxemia/heart failure closer to Hour 60. Still a nonspecific proxy with limited direct linkage to renal perfusion or tubular injury.
Minimum low-flow oxygen use in the immediate post-op period is largely driven by routine weaning/extubation practices. Very low renal specificity and high care-process dependence.
Minimum low-flow oxygen use in 13–24h mainly reflects whether the patient ever came off oxygen, a weaning/monitoring artifact. Weak and indirect relationship to creatinine trajectory.

Minimum low-flow oxygen use in 25–36h is primarily a marker of oxygen discontinuation practices rather than a physiologic insult. Limited renal relevance and redundant with other oxygen-device indicators.

Count/frequency of documenting low-flow oxygen in 0–12h reflects monitoring intensity and routine post-op charting. Measurement frequency is not a renal mechanism and is heavily confounded by workflow.
Documentation frequency of low-flow oxygen in 13–24h is mainly a care/monitoring proxy. At best weakly correlates with instability, with little direct prognostic proximity to AKI.
Measurement frequency in 25–36h remains a clinician-behavior/monitoring signal rather than physiology. Even near the outcome window it is too indirect to be useful for creatinine ratio prediction.

Indicates use of high-flow oxygen early post-op, suggesting more significant respiratory support needs. Renal relevance is indirect via severity/hemodynamics, and early timing is prone to transient post-bypass effects.

High-flow oxygen requirement in 13–24h suggests persistent respiratory/cardiac dysfunction and higher overall physiologic stress. This can correlate with renal hypoperfusion risk, though it remains nonspecific and treatment-influenced.

Ongoing high-flow oxygen use in 25–36h is a closer-in-time marker of unresolved cardiopulmonary compromise, which can accompany low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. Still indirect and partly a clinician-titration proxy, so not top-tier.
Any/peak high-flow oxygen use in 0–12h flags early respiratory support escalation but may be transient and protocol-driven. Indirect renal linkage through severity rather than a direct AKI pathway.

Any/peak high-flow oxygen use in 13–24h suggests clinically meaningful respiratory compromise beyond the immediate post-op period. Moderately predictive as a severity/low-output proxy that can precede worsening renal function, though not kidney-specific.

Use of high-flow oxygen in the 25–36h window reflects respiratory failure/overall severity and possibly low cardiac output states. It is only indirectly linked to AKI risk (hypoxemia/shock proxy) and is not kidney-specific.

Early (0–12h) high-flow oxygen use is common post-op and often transient, making it a weak and nonspecific proxy for later renal dysfunction. Minimal renal specificity and strong confounding by routine postoperative respiratory support.

Persistent need for high-flow oxygen by 13–24h suggests ongoing cardiopulmonary dysfunction and higher illness severity, which can correlate with AKI. Still indirect and not a renal mechanism, so predictive value is limited.

Ongoing high-flow oxygen requirement close to the outcome window may track persistent hypoxemia/low output and thus higher AKI risk. However it remains a nonspecific severity marker rather than a direct renal perfusion or injury signal.

Measurement count mainly reflects documentation/monitoring intensity rather than physiology. In the immediate post-op period this is especially protocol-driven, with little renal-specific information.
This is primarily a care-process/monitoring-frequency proxy and not a direct marker of renal perfusion or injury. Any association with creatinine change would be indirect via overall acuity.

More frequent recording of high-flow oxygen status at 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability and clinician concern, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a monitoring-intensity proxy, so capped at low importance.

Count of documented first-degree AV block is largely monitoring/ECG interpretation frequency and not kidney-specific. Early post-op conduction changes are common and only weakly related to renal outcomes.

This measured-frequency feature is a proxy for surveillance rather than a physiologic driver of AKI. First-degree block itself rarely causes meaningful hemodynamic compromise affecting renal perfusion.

Documentation frequency of first-degree AV block near 25–36h is still not a renal biomarker and usually has minimal hemodynamic impact. Any signal would be indirect and dominated by monitoring practices.

Measurement count of higher-grade AV block may reflect clinically significant conduction issues that can reduce cardiac output and renal perfusion, but this variable is still a monitoring proxy. In 0–12h, transient post-op blocks are common, limiting specificity.

Ongoing documentation of 2nd/3rd-degree block at 13–24h suggests persistent conduction problems and potential low-output states, which can increase AKI risk. However the '_measured' aggregation is indirect and confounded by clinician attention.

Frequent documentation of high-grade AV block in the 25–36h window may indicate persistent hemodynamically important arrhythmia/pacing needs, a plausible near-term precursor to renal hypoperfusion. Still not kidney-specific and is partly a care-process signal, so not higher.

Bundle branch block documentation frequency is largely an ECG monitoring artifact and often reflects baseline conduction disease rather than acute hemodynamic compromise. Weak and nonspecific linkage to postoperative creatinine rise.

This '_measured' feature mainly captures monitoring intensity; BBB itself is usually not a direct driver of renal hypoperfusion. Any association with AKI would be indirect via comorbidity/overall cardiac disease.

Near-term BBB documentation frequency remains a nonspecific process measure with low renal specificity. It is unlikely to add independent predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio beyond other hemodynamic variables.

Mean ionized calcium in the immediate postop period reflects citrate load/transfusion, CPB effects, and overall physiologic stress more than kidney-specific injury. Early mean values can wash out brief extremes and are only indirectly linked to later creatinine rise.

Ionized calcium mean in 13–24h may reflect ongoing critical illness, transfusion, or endocrine/metabolic derangements rather than a direct AKI pathway. Trajectory window is somewhat informative but still an indirect proxy for renal outcomes.

Later ionized calcium mean can correlate with severity of illness and ongoing resuscitation but is not a direct marker of renal perfusion or tubular injury. Mean aggregation remains relatively nonspecific for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Peak ionized calcium early after surgery is usually driven by supplementation practices and lab timing rather than renal physiology. It has weak mechanistic linkage to subsequent creatinine change and high clinician-behavior/proxy content.

Maximum ionized calcium in this window often reflects replacement intensity and monitoring rather than kidney injury. It is a nonspecific signal with limited prognostic proximity to AKI compared with hemodynamics/creatinine/urine output.

Late peak ionized calcium is still more indicative of supplementation and metabolic management than renal dysfunction. Despite closer timing, it remains a weak and behavior-influenced predictor of creatinine ratio.

Low ionized calcium early can indicate citrate toxicity from transfusion/CPB and severe physiologic derangement, which may co-occur with shock states that risk AKI. However it is indirect and early transient abnormalities are common postoperatively.

Persistently low ionized calcium in 13–24h may reflect ongoing critical illness or massive transfusion, which can track with AKI risk. Still, it is not kidney-specific and is an indirect severity marker.

A low ionized calcium minimum closer to the outcome may capture ongoing instability (e.g., continued transfusion/citrate load, poor perfusion) that can accompany evolving AKI. The minimum better preserves clinically meaningful extremes than the mean, though renal specificity remains moderate.
Measurement count mainly reflects monitoring intensity and clinician concern in the immediate postop period. It is a weak, nonspecific proxy and not a physiologic mechanism for creatinine rise.

Frequency of ionized calcium checks is largely a care-process variable (lab ordering/acuity) rather than renal pathophysiology. Proxy penalty is high and predictive value for creatinine ratio is limited.

More frequent ionized calcium measurement late may signal ongoing instability or transfusion needs, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still, it is primarily monitoring-intensity and should not be weighted highly.

Mean magnesium early after cardiac surgery reflects replacement practices, hemodilution, and overall illness severity more than kidney injury. Renal handling affects magnesium, but early mean values are indirect and often treatment-confounded.

Magnesium mean in 13–24h can reflect renal excretion capacity and ongoing supplementation, but is heavily influenced by clinician replacement protocols. It is a plausible but weak predictor of later creatinine ratio.

Later magnesium mean may better reflect reduced renal clearance (especially if supplementation is stable), making it a modest leading indicator of evolving renal dysfunction. Still, confounding by replacement limits renal specificity, so it is not top-tier.

Peak magnesium in the immediate postop window is commonly driven by bolus replacement and lab timing rather than intrinsic renal injury. It has high proxy/behavior influence and weak direct linkage to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Peak magnesium in the 13–24h window mainly reflects supplementation, shifts, or reduced clearance rather than a specific AKI pathway. It is an indirect and treatment-influenced signal with only modest renal specificity.

Later peak magnesium could weakly reflect declining renal clearance closer to the outcome window, but is still heavily confounded by replacement practices. Renal specificity is limited and it is not a strong near-term AKI biomarker.

Early postoperative nadir magnesium is commonly driven by hemodilution, bypass-related shifts, and replacement timing. It has low renal specificity and poor prognostic proximity for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
A low magnesium nadir in 13–24h more often reflects ongoing repletion needs or redistribution than kidney injury. It is an indirect proxy with weak linkage to subsequent creatinine rise.

Low magnesium late is still more consistent with inadequate replacement/redistribution than impaired renal function (which would tend to raise Mg). Minimal renal specificity for predicting creatinine_ratio.
Measurement count is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy rather than physiology. Early postoperative labs are routine, so it adds little renal-specific predictive value.
Frequency of magnesium checks mostly reflects ICU protocol or clinician concern and is not a direct renal mechanism. At best it is a weak proxy for instability, so low importance.

More frequent magnesium measurement late may indicate ongoing instability, diuretic use, or concern for renal/electrolyte issues, but remains a process-of-care proxy. Per rules, capped low despite closer timing.

Mean base excess reflects overall metabolic acid-base status and global perfusion/oxygen debt, which can contribute to AKI risk but is nonspecific early after surgery. The mean may dilute short hypotensive/low-flow insults.

Persistent metabolic acidosis in 13–24h suggests ongoing hypoperfusion/shock or impaired clearance and is more prognostically informative than immediate post-op values. Still indirect for kidney injury and mean aggregation can wash out extremes.

Sustained abnormal base excess close to Hour 60 can indicate ongoing low-flow state or systemic illness that predisposes to worsening renal function. It is a moderate, indirect predictor rather than a renal-specific biomarker.

High (more positive) base excess early often reflects bicarbonate administration, transfusion/citrate effects, or ventilatory changes rather than renal physiology. Low renal specificity and strong treatment confounding.
A high base excess peak is usually iatrogenic/compensatory and not a typical AKI precursor compared with negative extremes. Limited renal relevance and poor mechanistic linkage to creatinine rise.

Even late, a positive base excess peak is more reflective of buffering/therapy than renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI. Weak prognostic proximity for creatinine_ratio compared with base deficit measures.

Worst (most negative) base excess captures peak metabolic acidosis/base deficit, a better marker of low-flow state and oxygen debt that can precipitate AKI. Early timing warrants some discount due to transient post-bypass derangements.

A markedly negative base excess in 13–24h indicates persistent metabolic acidosis and ongoing hypoperfusion/shock, a strong near-term precursor for AKI after cardiac surgery. The minimum aggregation appropriately captures the clinically meaningful extreme.

Most negative base excess near 36h reflects worst metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion/lactate or impaired renal acid handling, both linked to evolving AKI. The late-window minimum captures clinically meaningful extremes close to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome.

Count/frequency of base excess measurements mainly reflects monitoring intensity immediately post-op rather than renal physiology. Early post-bypass instability drives labs and is a weak, proxy signal for later creatinine rise.
Measurement frequency is largely a clinician-behavior proxy (concern/acuity) rather than a kidney-specific mechanism. Mid-window timing adds little direct information about Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

More frequent base excess checks late may indicate ongoing shock/acidosis workup, which can correlate with AKI risk, but it remains a monitoring-intensity proxy. Cannot be a top predictor because it does not encode the physiologic derangement itself.

Direct bilirubin measurement frequency reflects lab ordering/monitoring rather than renal injury pathways. In the immediate post-op period it is especially nonspecific for predicting creatinine at 60h.
This is primarily a care-process/monitoring proxy (evaluation of hepatic dysfunction/hemolysis) with weak kidney specificity. Mid-epoch timing does not make it a strong predictor of creatinine ratio.

Late increased bilirubin monitoring may mark persistent multiorgan dysfunction, which can co-occur with AKI, but it is still indirect and behavior-driven. Thus it provides at most weak incremental signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Mean WBC early post-op reflects inflammatory/stress response and possible infection risk but is not kidney-specific. The mean in the immediate window is noisy after surgery and only weakly prognostic for later creatinine rise.
Mid-window mean WBC may indicate evolving inflammation/infection that can contribute to AKI, but remains indirect. Averaging can dilute clinically important spikes, limiting predictive strength.

Sustained leukocytosis closer to the outcome can reflect infection/SIRS and ongoing critical illness that increases AKI risk. Late timing improves prognostic proximity, though it is still an indirect renal predictor.

Peak WBC early may capture acute inflammatory response to surgery/CPB, which is common and nonspecific for AKI. Early timing and confounding by operative stress limit its usefulness for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

A higher WBC peak in the trajectory phase can signal infection/SIRS or ongoing inflammation, which is associated with AKI risk. Using the maximum better captures clinically meaningful extremes than the mean.
Late peak WBC is closer to the creatinine outcome and may indicate infection/SIRS driving renal dysfunction. Still indirect and prone to confounding, so it is not a top-tier renal predictor.
Low WBC early is uncommon and often reflects dilutional effects or lab variability rather than a renal mechanism. It has weak and nonspecific linkage to subsequent creatinine rise.
Minimum WBC is generally less informative than peaks for inflammatory burden and is not kidney-specific. Any association with AKI would be indirect and likely weak.

Late low WBC may reflect dilution, marrow suppression, or measurement noise and does not directly track renal perfusion or injury. As an extreme in the low direction it is typically not a meaningful AKI precursor.

Frequency of WBC checks mainly reflects monitoring intensity/clinical concern rather than a renal mechanism. Early postop measurement patterns are especially process-driven and weakly tied to Hour-60 creatinine.
WBC measurement count is a care-process proxy (infection/severity surveillance) with low renal specificity. Slightly later timing doesn’t overcome the strong proxy penalty.

Later WBC monitoring frequency may signal evolving sepsis/inflammation prompting closer surveillance, which can relate indirectly to AKI risk. Still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy, so capped low.

Mean PT reflects coagulation status (liver dysfunction, dilutional coagulopathy, anticoagulation) and overall physiologic stress, not kidney function directly. In the immediate postop window it is often transient and less prognostic for later creatinine rise.

Persistently prolonged mean PT by 25–36h can indicate ongoing hepatic dysfunction, shock, or consumptive coagulopathy, which correlates with multiorgan dysfunction and AKI risk. Mean may dilute extremes but sustained abnormality can still be informative near the outcome.

Peak PT early after surgery often reflects intraop anticoagulation effects, transfusion/dilution, or transient hepatic hypoperfusion. It is an indirect severity marker with limited renal specificity in the 0–12h epoch.

A high PT maximum at 25–36h suggests episodic or worsening coagulopathy from shock/liver dysfunction, which is associated with higher AKI risk. Using the max captures clinically meaningful extremes better than the mean, but remains indirect for renal injury.

Low/normal PT minimum is generally not a plausible driver of AKI and mostly reflects baseline/assay variation or anticoagulation reversal. Minimal renal specificity and limited prognostic value for creatinine ratio.
PT minimum in this window largely captures the least abnormal value and is unlikely to encode renal hypoperfusion or injury. Redundant and weak compared with PT max/mean for severity signaling.

PT measurement frequency is primarily a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy (bleeding/anticoagulation management) rather than a physiologic renal signal. Early postop testing is routine and poorly specific for later creatinine rise.
Ongoing PT check frequency reflects care intensity and anticoagulation/bleeding concerns more than kidney pathophysiology. Proxy penalty dominates despite being in the trajectory phase.
More frequent PT checks late may indicate persistent instability (bleeding, liver dysfunction, shock) that can co-occur with AKI. Still a monitoring-intensity proxy and therefore kept low.

NRBCs indicate severe physiologic stress/hypoxemia/inflammation and correlate with critical illness severity, which can relate indirectly to AKI. In the immediate postop period this may be transient and nonspecific for renal outcomes.

Persisting NRBC elevation in 13–24h suggests ongoing hypoxia/shock or systemic inflammation, which are established AKI risk contexts. Mean is acceptable for sustained presence but remains an indirect marker.

NRBCs remaining elevated close to the outcome window signal continued severe stress or hypoperfusion, increasing likelihood of evolving AKI and higher creatinine ratio. Still not kidney-specific, so not top-tier.
Peak NRBCs early may capture an acute intraop/postop hypoxic or shock insult, which can predispose to AKI. However, early extremes can be transient and NRBCs are a nonspecific severity marker.

Peak NRBCs in the 13–24h window suggests systemic stress/hypoxemia/inflammation after surgery. It is an indirect severity marker with limited renal specificity, though persistent elevation can correlate with multiorgan dysfunction and AKI risk.

Peak NRBCs closer to the outcome window may reflect ongoing hypoperfusion or critical illness that can precede AKI. Still nonspecific to kidney injury, but temporal proximity makes it a somewhat stronger prognostic signal.
Minimum NRBCs early post-op is unlikely to capture a renal-relevant insult and is often near zero. This is a weak, nonspecific marker with poor mechanistic linkage to creatinine rise.
Minimum NRBCs in 13–24h is not clinically meaningful for AKI prediction compared with peaks or persistence. It provides little renal specificity and is largely redundant/noisy.

Minimum NRBCs in the 25–36h window does not represent hypoxic burden or inflammatory stress well (extremes in the opposite direction matter more). Minimal renal specificity and low aggregation utility.
Count of NRBC measurements mainly reflects lab ordering/monitoring intensity rather than physiology. Per rules, this is a care-process proxy with little direct renal relevance.

Measurement frequency is primarily clinician behavior and correlates with general instability rather than kidney-specific pathways. Not appropriate for high importance for creatinine ratio prediction.
More frequent NRBC checks late may indicate heightened concern for ongoing critical illness, which can track with AKI risk. Still a monitoring-intensity proxy, so capped low.

Average glucose immediately post-op reflects stress hyperglycemia and insulin management, which are indirect AKI risk correlates. Early means can be confounded by bypass/anesthesia and may wash out short extremes.

Mean glucose in 13–24h reflects ongoing metabolic stress and glycemic control, modestly associated with complications including AKI. It remains indirect and influenced by treatment, limiting renal specificity.

Sustained dysglycemia closer to Hour 60 can indicate persistent stress, infection, or shock physiology that increases AKI risk. Still not kidney-specific, but temporal proximity and sustained exposure make it a moderate predictor.
Peak glucose early post-op captures stress hyperglycemia but is often transient and heavily treatment-influenced. It is an indirect marker with limited renal mechanistic specificity.
Peak glucose in 13–24h may better reflect persistent stress response or evolving complications, which can precede AKI. Max aggregation captures clinically meaningful extremes more than the mean.

Late peak glucose is closer to the creatinine outcome and may signal ongoing critical illness/infection or high catecholamine/vasopressor states linked to AKI. Still indirect and partly treatment-driven, so not top-tier.

Early hypoglycemia can reflect over-insulinization or hepatic dysfunction and may associate with severe illness, but it is not kidney-specific. Min aggregation captures a potentially harmful extreme, though early timing limits prognostic value.

Minimum glucose in 13–24h captures hypoglycemic episodes that may indicate tight control or severe systemic illness. Association with AKI is indirect and confounded by clinician management, keeping importance low-moderate.

Minimum glucose in the 25–36h window reflects hypoglycemia/over-tight control and overall stress response rather than a kidney-specific pathway. It may weakly track severity and catecholamine/insulin use near the outcome, but is an indirect predictor of creatinine rise.

Frequency of glucose checks in the immediate postop period is primarily a monitoring-intensity/insulin-protocol proxy. It has little direct mechanistic linkage to AKI or creatinine ratio and is heavily confounded by care processes.

Glucose measurement count mainly reflects clinician attention, diabetes/insulin infusion protocols, or instability rather than renal physiology. As a process variable it is a weak, indirect signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

More frequent glucose monitoring at 25–36h can indicate ongoing critical illness or insulin infusion closer to the outcome window. Still, it is largely a care-process proxy and not kidney-specific, so predictive value should be limited.

Mean platelet count early after cardiac surgery reflects hemodilution/CPB effect and general inflammatory/coagulopathic state. It is only indirectly related to renal injury pathways and early means can wash out clinically relevant nadirs.

Average platelets in 13–24h may reflect evolving inflammation/consumption and overall severity, which can correlate with AKI risk. However it remains nonspecific and the mean is less informative than a nadir for acute complications.

Persistently lower mean platelets at 25–36h can signal ongoing systemic inflammation/consumption or complications (e.g., sepsis, bleeding) that increase AKI risk near the outcome window. Still indirect for kidney function, so not top-tier.

Maximum platelet count in the immediate postop window is rarely pathologic and mostly reflects baseline/measurement noise. It has minimal renal specificity and little prognostic proximity to creatinine rise.
A platelet maximum in 13–24h is generally not a meaningful marker of renal perfusion or kidney injury. It is largely redundant and clinically uninformative for AKI prediction.
Platelet maxima at 25–36h typically do not capture the consumptive/coagulopathic processes that would relate to AKI. As an extreme in the non-pathologic direction, it offers little predictive signal.

Early platelet nadir can reflect CPB-related consumption/hemodilution and early coagulopathy, which correlate with postoperative complications and AKI risk. Timing is early and may be transient, so it is a moderate rather than strong predictor.

A low platelet nadir in 13–24h suggests ongoing consumption/inflammation or bleeding, which is associated with higher AKI risk. It is still indirect for renal injury but more prognostically meaningful than the mean.

A low platelet nadir at 25–36h is a strong marker of ongoing systemic illness/consumption (e.g., sepsis, DIC-like physiology, major bleeding/transfusion) close to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. While not kidney-specific, its temporal proximity and extreme aggregation make it a strong predictor of subsequent renal dysfunction.
Platelet measurement frequency early postop mainly reflects routine lab schedules, bleeding concern, or monitoring intensity. It is a process proxy with low renal specificity.

Count of platelet measurements in 13–24h is primarily driven by clinician behavior (bleeding/transfusion workup) rather than renal mechanisms. As a monitoring proxy it should contribute little to creatinine ratio prediction.

More frequent platelet checks at 25–36h may indicate ongoing bleeding/coagulopathy or instability closer to the outcome window. Nonetheless it remains a monitoring-intensity proxy and should not be weighted highly.

Mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) reflects RBC indices/anemia phenotype rather than renal function or perfusion. The mean in the immediate postop window is unlikely to add specific signal for Hour-60 creatinine beyond nonspecific illness context.

MCH mean is a stable hematologic index with weak mechanistic linkage to AKI compared with hemodynamics, lactate, urine output, or creatinine. Mid-epoch timing does not make it a meaningful leading indicator for creatinine rise.
Even close to the outcome, MCH mean mainly reflects anemia/macrocytosis and transfusion history rather than evolving kidney injury. It is not a direct renal biomarker and is largely nonspecific.
Peak MCH over 0–12h is typically driven by lab variability or transfusion-related shifts, not renal perfusion or tubular injury. Extreme aggregation here does not capture a known AKI pathway.
Maximum MCH in this window has minimal renal specificity and is not a recognized precursor of postoperative AKI. Any association would be indirect via overall illness or transfusion practices.

Late-window MCH max remains a nonspecific hematology metric with little prognostic proximity to creatinine ratio. It is unlikely to contribute meaningfully in a sparse model compared with renal/hemodynamic variables.

Minimum MCH early after surgery may reflect dilution/transfusion effects rather than kidney injury mechanisms. The extreme does not map to renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxic exposure in a specific way.
Low MCH suggests chronic microcytosis/iron deficiency more than acute renal risk. It is a weak, indirect proxy at best for postoperative creatinine rise.
Late low MCH still reflects baseline hematologic status and is not a near-term biomarker of AKI. Any predictive value would be marginal and nonspecific.

The count of MCH measurements mainly reflects CBC ordering frequency/monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. Per rules, _measured variables are strong proxies for clinician behavior and should score low.
Measurement frequency of MCH is a care-process signal (how often CBCs were checked) with little kidney specificity. It may correlate with acuity but not directly with creatinine ratio.
Even in the closest window, MCH measurement count is primarily monitoring intensity and not a mechanistic AKI indicator. It should not be prioritized over physiologic renal predictors.

Mean serum sodium reflects fluid balance, dilution, and neurohormonal stress, which can correlate indirectly with AKI risk. In the immediate postop period it is often transiently affected by bypass/resuscitation, limiting specificity.

Sodium mean in the trajectory phase can reflect ongoing free-water balance and severity of illness, with modest indirect association to renal dysfunction. It is not a direct kidney injury marker and the mean may dilute short extremes.

Later sodium mean may capture persistent dysnatremia from fluid management and evolving renal handling, giving some prognostic value. Still, it is indirect and nonspecific compared with creatinine/urine output/hemodynamics.

Peak sodium early may indicate hypernatremia from diuresis, sodium load, or free-water deficit, which can accompany renal hypoperfusion but is not specific. The max captures extremes better than mean, yet early timing remains confounded by perioperative fluids.

Peak sodium in the 13–24h window reflects dysnatremia from fluids/diuretics and overall homeostasis rather than kidney injury per se. It is an indirect, nonspecific signal with moderate temporal proximity.

Late peak sodium may capture ongoing free-water deficit, diuretic effect, or hypertonic exposure, which can correlate with illness severity and renal concentrating issues. Still indirect for AKI/creatinine rise, so modest importance despite closer timing.

Early postoperative nadir sodium often reflects hemodilution, bypass prime, and resuscitation rather than evolving AKI. Extremes can matter, but in 0–12h this is commonly transient and nonspecific for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Nadir sodium in 13–24h may indicate ongoing fluid overload or SIADH-like physiology and can track severity/volume status. Renal linkage is indirect and confounded by clinician-driven fluids, so weak-to-moderate predictor.

Late nadir sodium can reflect persistent positive balance or impaired water handling and may co-occur with renal dysfunction. However it is not a direct AKI biomarker and is heavily treatment-influenced, limiting predictive specificity.
Whether sodium was measured in 0–12h mainly reflects routine postoperative lab cadence/monitoring intensity. It is a care-process proxy with minimal mechanistic linkage to subsequent creatinine rise.

Sodium measurement frequency in 13–24h is largely driven by ICU protocols and clinician behavior rather than renal pathophysiology. As a monitoring proxy, it has low expected value for predicting creatinine_ratio.

In 25–36h, continued sodium checks may signal ongoing instability, diuresis, or electrolyte management that can accompany evolving AKI. Still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy, so capped at low importance.

TEG platelet aggregation being measured early is mainly a marker of bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation and transfusion planning. It has little direct renal specificity and is largely clinician-behavior driven.
Measurement indicates ongoing concern for hemostasis rather than kidney injury. Any association with AKI would be indirect via severity/transfusion, so low importance.

Late TEG platelet aggregation measurement remains a process-of-care indicator for bleeding/thrombosis management. It is not a renal biomarker and is unlikely to add specific signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Mean MCHC reflects red cell indices/hemodilution and is not specific to renal perfusion or tubular injury. In the immediate postoperative window it is especially confounded by fluids and transfusion, limiting relevance.
Average MCHC in 13–24h is a nonspecific hematologic index that may weakly track dilution/transfusion. It has minimal mechanistic connection to AKI and thus low predictive utility.

Late mean MCHC remains a largely irrelevant RBC index for renal outcomes, with any association mediated through nonspecific illness or transfusion patterns. Not a meaningful predictor of creatinine_ratio.

Peak MCHC is typically driven by lab artifact, hemoconcentration, or transfusion effects rather than renal injury pathways. Early postoperative extremes are not reliably informative for later creatinine rise.
Max MCHC in 13–24h is still a nonspecific hematology extreme with limited physiologic linkage to AKI. It is unlikely to add renal-specific prognostic information beyond general care context.

MCHC reflects red cell hemoglobin concentration and is not a direct marker of renal function or perfusion. Even in the closest window, its max value is usually nonspecific and weakly related to AKI risk.

Low MCHC early post-op is typically related to anemia/hemolysis/lab variation rather than kidney injury mechanisms. The immediate 0–12h period is noisy and transient, limiting prognostic value for Hour-60 creatinine.
Minimum MCHC in 13–24h remains a nonspecific hematology index with little renal specificity. It may correlate weakly with overall illness or transfusion/hemolysis but is not a strong AKI precursor.

Late low MCHC is still not mechanistically tied to renal hypoperfusion or tubular injury. Any association would be indirect (anemia/bleeding/transfusion context) and likely redundant with more informative hemodynamic/lab features.

A “measured” count mainly reflects monitoring intensity rather than physiology. Early post-op lab frequency is routine after cardiac surgery and provides minimal specific signal for subsequent creatinine rise.
Measurement frequency is largely a care-process proxy and not kidney-specific. In this setting it is usually driven by standard ICU protocols rather than targeted renal concern.

More frequent labs in the 25–36h window can weakly proxy clinical instability or concern (including evolving AKI), but it remains a clinician-behavior signal. Per rules, measured variables should stay low despite temporal proximity.

Mean hematocrit reflects anemia/hemodilution and can influence renal oxygen delivery, but early post-op values are heavily affected by bypass prime and resuscitation. As a mean, it may miss brief severe anemia/hypoperfusion episodes relevant to AKI.

Hematocrit in 13–24h may better reflect ongoing bleeding/hemodilution and transfusion needs, which can relate indirectly to renal risk. Still, it is nonspecific and mean aggregation dilutes extremes that matter more for renal ischemia.

Persistently low hematocrit closer to the outcome can indicate sustained anemia/hemodilution and reduced oxygen delivery, which may contribute to renal vulnerability in older post-cardiac surgery patients. Timing is favorable, though it remains an indirect predictor.

A high hematocrit early post-op often reflects hemoconcentration or transfusion effects and is not a typical AKI pathway signal. In the immediate post-op window it is particularly confounded by fluid shifts and does not reliably predict creatinine rise.

Maximum hematocrit is generally less relevant to AKI than low hematocrit, and may reflect transfusion or diuresis rather than renal injury. Its association with Hour-60 creatinine ratio is likely weak and indirect.

Late hemoconcentration could weakly suggest relative hypovolemia/diuresis and reduced renal perfusion, but this is indirect and confounded by clinician management. Max hematocrit is less informative than minima for renal oxygen delivery risk.

Minimum hematocrit captures worst anemia/hemodilution, which can contribute to renal ischemia risk, but early post-op nadirs are common and often transient. Temporal noise in 0–12h limits its predictive strength.

A low hematocrit nadir in 13–24h may reflect ongoing bleeding/hemodilution and higher transfusion/instability burden, which are established AKI risk contexts. Using the minimum better captures clinically meaningful extremes than the mean.

A low hematocrit nadir in the 25–36h window is a closer-in-time marker of sustained anemia/hemodilution and potential impaired renal oxygen delivery. It is still indirect (not a renal biomarker) but the timing and extreme aggregation improve usefulness.

Counts how often hematocrit was checked in the first 12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity/transfusion concern rather than renal physiology. As a _measured variable it is a weak proxy for acuity and not a direct AKI pathway signal.

Frequency of hematocrit measurement in 13–24h is primarily clinician behavior/monitoring intensity. It has minimal direct linkage to renal perfusion or evolving kidney injury and is penalized as _measured.

Later (25–36h) hematocrit check frequency may weakly proxy ongoing bleeding/transfusion or instability closer to the creatinine outcome. Still, it is a monitoring-intensity variable with indirect renal relevance, so it remains low.

Mean basophil count is a nonspecific CBC differential component with little mechanistic connection to AKI after cardiac surgery. Early postoperative means are especially noisy and unlikely to add renal-specific predictive value.
Basophil mean in 13–24h remains a nonspecific inflammatory/allergic marker without established prognostic proximity to postoperative creatinine rise. Any association would be indirect and weak.

Peak basophil count in 0–12h is unlikely to capture a renal injury pathway and is often driven by lab variability rather than true pathology. Extremes here do not meaningfully encode renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxic exposure.
Maximum basophil count in 13–24h is still not renal-specific and has poor causal/prognostic proximity to AKI. Even as an extreme, it is not a clinically meaningful kidney signal.

Minimum basophil count early post-op is largely noise/assay floor effects and not tied to renal function or perfusion. It offers negligible incremental information for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Minimum basophil count in 13–24h has no established relationship to evolving AKI and is likely redundant/noisy. Renal specificity and prognostic proximity are minimal.
Number of basophil measurements reflects CBC ordering frequency rather than a kidney mechanism. As a _measured feature it is primarily a care-process proxy with low renal relevance.

CBC differential measurement frequency in 13–24h is monitoring intensity and not a direct indicator of renal injury. Any signal would be indirect via overall illness severity, so importance is minimal.

Even close to the outcome window, basophil measurement frequency mainly reflects clinician ordering patterns rather than renal physiology. _Measured variables are penalized and should not be high for AKI prediction.

Mean lymphocyte percentage is a nonspecific marker of stress/inflammation (e.g., postoperative lymphopenia) that may correlate weakly with overall severity. Renal linkage is indirect and early timing is prone to transient postoperative effects.

Lymphocyte percent in 13–24h may reflect ongoing systemic inflammation or stress response, which can correlate with AKI risk indirectly. However it is not renal-specific and the mean can dilute short clinically relevant insults.

Maximum lymphocyte percentage in 0–12h is still a nonspecific immune/stress signal and may be influenced by hemodilution/leukocyte shifts after bypass. It has only weak, indirect prognostic value for creatinine rise.

Peak lymphocyte percentage in 13–24h could weakly track recovery vs persistent stress/inflammation, which may correlate with AKI risk. Nonetheless it remains indirect and not a strong renal mechanism marker.

Minimum lymphocyte percentage early post-op mainly reflects stress response/inflammation or dilution rather than renal physiology. Weak and nonspecific for AKI prediction, and early transient changes are common after surgery.
Low lymphocyte percentage in the 13–24h trajectory window can reflect ongoing systemic inflammation/sepsis risk, which can secondarily relate to AKI. Still indirect with low renal specificity.
Whether lymphocyte percent was measured is primarily a monitoring-intensity/lab-ordering proxy. It does not encode a renal mechanism and is especially noisy in the immediate post-op period.
Measurement flag reflects clinician behavior and overall acuity rather than kidney injury pathways. Per rules, _measured variables are low value for predicting creatinine ratio.
In the closest window, repeated CBC differentials may signal concern for infection/inflammation that can contribute to AKI risk. Still largely a process-of-care proxy, so capped low.

Ordering a random vancomycin level suggests vancomycin exposure and/or concern for altered clearance, which can relate to renal function. However it is a measurement/proxy variable and early timing limits specificity.

Random vancomycin level measurement may indicate ongoing vancomycin therapy and clinician concern about drug accumulation from reduced GFR. Still indirect and confounded by infection severity and monitoring practices.

In the 25–36h leading window, checking random vancomycin levels can be a stronger signal of evolving reduced clearance/AKI concern. Nonetheless it remains a clinician-behavior proxy rather than a direct renal biomarker.

Trough measurement implies vancomycin exposure and dosing/clearance monitoring, which can correlate with renal dysfunction risk. As a _measured flag in the immediate post-op period, it is still mostly process-driven.

Trough monitoring in 13–24h may reflect concern for impaired clearance and nephrotoxin management. Predictive value is limited by confounding from infection severity and the fact it is not the level itself.

Nearer to the outcome, trough monitoring can more plausibly indicate reduced renal clearance prompting closer drug monitoring. Still capped because it reflects ordering behavior rather than measured nephrotoxic exposure or kidney function.

Mean oxygen saturation reflects overall oxygenation and cardiopulmonary stability, which can influence renal oxygen delivery indirectly. Early post-op means can mask brief hypoxemic insults and are nonspecific for AKI.

Sustained oxygenation in the trajectory phase may relate to ongoing respiratory/cardiac dysfunction and systemic hypoxia affecting kidneys. Still indirect and mean aggregation may dilute clinically important desaturations.

In the closest window, persistently lower mean SpO2 can indicate ongoing hypoxemia/low reserve that may contribute to renal hypoxia and AKI risk. It remains an indirect marker and less informative than hemodynamics or direct renal labs.

Maximum SpO2 is usually near 100% and reflects supplemental oxygen/ventilator settings rather than pathology. It has minimal renal specificity and poor aggregation quality for capturing hypoxemic injury.
Max SpO2 largely reflects oxygen delivery strategy and is typically non-discriminatory. It is not a meaningful predictor of creatinine rise compared with minima or sustained hypoxemia measures.
Peak SpO2 in the 25–36h window mainly reflects adequate oxygenation and is rarely the limiting factor for renal perfusion. Max aggregation is not informative for AKI risk and is largely nonspecific.

Early postoperative nadir SpO2 can reflect hypoxemia during immediate post-op instability, which may contribute indirectly to renal ischemic stress. However 0–12h abnormalities are often transient and nonspecific for later creatinine rise.

A low SpO2 nadir in 13–24h suggests ongoing hypoxemia/respiratory failure and higher illness severity, which can secondarily worsen renal oxygen delivery. Still an indirect proxy rather than a kidney-specific marker.

A low SpO2 nadir close to the outcome window indicates persistent hypoxemia, which can plausibly impair renal oxygenation and correlate with evolving multi-organ dysfunction. Temporal proximity and extreme-value aggregation make it a moderate predictor despite low renal specificity.
Measurement count primarily reflects monitoring intensity and workflow rather than physiology. It is a weak, proxy signal with high clinician-behavior confounding.
Frequency of SpO2 recording is mostly a care-process/acuity proxy and not a direct renal mechanism. Not expected to add stable predictive value beyond actual SpO2 values.
More frequent SpO2 measurements in 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability or ventilatory support, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still largely a monitoring-intensity proxy, so capped low.

Mean ALT in the immediate post-op period reflects hepatic injury or hypoperfusion (“shock liver”) and overall ischemic burden. It is indirect for kidney injury and the mean may dilute brief but important peaks.

Peak ALT early after surgery can capture acute hepatic ischemia from low-flow states, which often co-tracks with renal hypoperfusion risk. Max aggregation better captures insult severity, but timing is early and renal specificity is limited.
Minimum ALT has little clinical meaning for organ injury and does not capture hypoperfusion severity. It is unlikely to be informative for subsequent creatinine rise.
ALT measurement frequency is mainly a clinician-suspicion/monitoring proxy rather than a physiologic driver of AKI. High proxy penalty and weak renal linkage.
How often ALT is checked reflects care patterns and concern for liver injury, not kidney function. Adds little mechanistic information for predicting creatinine ratio.
ALT measurement count in 25–36h remains primarily a monitoring-intensity signal with substantial confounding. Not a direct renal biomarker and should not score highly.

BUN reflects renal clearance and catabolic/volume status; early postoperative elevation can indicate reduced renal perfusion or pre-renal physiology. However 0–12h values can be influenced by hemodilution, transfusion, and perioperative fluids.

BUN in 13–24h better reflects evolving renal dysfunction or sustained pre-renal azotemia after initial resuscitation. As a kidney-related lab with reasonable temporal proximity, it is a strong predictor though less specific than creatinine.

BUN in 25–36h is a near-term marker of impaired renal clearance and/or ongoing hypoperfusion close to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. Strong renal relevance and temporal proximity make it highly predictive despite some nonrenal influences.

Peak BUN early post-op reflects renal clearance and catabolic/volume status but is influenced by hemodilution, transfusion, and perioperative protein load. Moderately predictive of later creatinine rise, though early values can be transient and less specific than creatinine.

Peak BUN in the trajectory phase may capture evolving azotemia from reduced GFR or hypoperfusion. Still indirect and affected by nonrenal factors, but more informative than the immediate 0–12h window.

Peak BUN closer to Hour 60 is a stronger leading indicator of worsening renal clearance and impending AKI. The max aggregation captures clinically meaningful azotemia better than mean/min and has good temporal proximity.
Low BUN early post-op often reflects hemodilution or fluid loading rather than renal injury. The minimum is less aligned with AKI mechanisms and is a weaker predictor of later creatinine increase.
Minimum BUN in 13–24h is still more reflective of dilution/nutrition than renal dysfunction. Limited renal specificity and weaker prognostic proximity to creatinine rise than peak BUN.
Even near the outcome window, a low BUN is not a typical signal of AKI and is often driven by fluid balance or low protein intake. Min aggregation poorly captures renal injury risk compared with max.
Whether/how often BUN was measured mainly reflects monitoring intensity and clinician concern rather than a renal mechanism. Early post-op labs are routine, so this is a weak proxy.
BUN measurement frequency in this window is largely care-process driven and highly confounded by ICU practice patterns. It adds little direct information beyond the BUN values themselves.
More frequent BUN checks closer to Hour 60 may indicate evolving instability or suspected AKI, but it remains a proxy for clinician behavior. Kept low due to strong proxy penalty.

Amylase measurement is typically prompted by concern for pancreatitis or abdominal pathology, not kidney function. Any association with creatinine is indirect via overall illness severity and testing behavior.
Amylase being checked reflects clinician suspicion for nonrenal complications and monitoring intensity. It has minimal renal specificity and weak prognostic proximity to AKI.
Amylase measurement in this later window still primarily signals nonrenal diagnostic workup rather than renal physiology. Any predictive value would be nonspecific and proxy-driven.

Mean pH early post-op reflects acid-base status influenced by bypass, ventilation, and resuscitation; acidosis can correlate with hypoperfusion but is often transient. Mean aggregation may wash out brief severe acidosis relevant to renal ischemia.
Persistently low mean pH in 13–24h suggests ongoing shock, lactic acidosis, or renal acid retention, which are linked to AKI risk. This window better captures recovery vs deterioration than 0–12h.

Mean pH closer to Hour 60 reflects sustained acid-base derangement from ongoing hypoperfusion or declining renal function. Still indirect for GFR and mean may miss short severe episodes, limiting it from scoring higher.

High pH (alkalemia) early post-op is commonly driven by hyperventilation or bicarbonate administration and is not a typical AKI pathway signal. The max aggregation captures a state with low renal specificity for predicting creatinine rise.

Peak alkalemia in the 13–24h window is usually driven by ventilation/diuresis and is only indirectly related to renal outcomes. It is a nonspecific physiologic signal and less informative than acidemia extremes for AKI risk.
Peak alkalemia closer to Hour 60 may reflect ongoing hyperventilation or contraction alkalosis rather than kidney injury itself. Temporal proximity helps slightly, but renal specificity remains low.

Early postoperative nadir pH captures acidemia from hypoperfusion, lactate burden, or respiratory failure, which can contribute to renal ischemic risk. However, immediate post-bypass derangements are common/transient, so predictive value is moderate.

Nadir pH in 13–24h reflects persistent or recurrent acidemia (shock/sepsis/respiratory failure), a plausible precursor to AKI via hypoperfusion and inflammation. This trajectory window is more informative than 0–12h but still indirect.

Late-window nadir pH is a near-term marker of ongoing acidemia and physiologic instability, often correlating with poor perfusion and higher AKI risk. The extreme (min) captures clinically meaningful insults and is temporally close to the Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Whether pH was measured mainly reflects monitoring intensity and clinician concern rather than a renal mechanism. In the immediate postoperative period, frequent ABGs are routine and weakly discriminative for AKI prediction.
ABG/pH measurement frequency in 13–24h can proxy ongoing instability or ventilatory management, which may correlate with AKI risk. Still largely a care-process signal with limited renal specificity.

Continued pH measurement in 25–36h may indicate persistent critical illness or respiratory/hemodynamic issues closer to the outcome. Nonetheless it remains a monitoring proxy and should not score high.

Cholesterol measurement is unrelated to acute renal perfusion or kidney injury pathways in the early postoperative ICU course. This is primarily a low-value process/ordering artifact for predicting Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
Whether cholesterol was checked in 13–24h is not mechanistically linked to evolving AKI and mostly reflects non-renal clinical workflows. Predictive utility for creatinine rise is minimal.

Cholesterol testing in 25–36h remains a nonspecific ordering behavior with no direct connection to acute kidney function changes. It is unlikely to add meaningful signal in a sparse AKI-focused model.

Mean lymphocyte count early after surgery reflects stress response/inflammation and overall illness severity, which can correlate weakly with AKI. Renal specificity is low and early values are often transiently suppressed.
Mean lymphocyte count in 13–24h may indicate ongoing inflammatory stress or infection risk, indirectly associated with AKI. It remains nonspecific and not a direct renal biomarker.
Maximum lymphocyte count in the immediate postoperative window is rarely a meaningful pathophysiologic driver of AKI and is prone to noise. It adds little beyond general lab variability.
A higher lymphocyte maximum in 13–24h is not a recognized near-term precursor of postoperative creatinine rise. This aggregation is unlikely to capture relevant renal-risk physiology.

Minimum lymphocyte count early post-op can reflect stress leukogram/catecholamine effect and systemic inflammation, which may weakly track AKI risk. However it is indirect and confounded by perioperative factors and dilution.

Minimum lymphocyte count reflects stress/inflammation or hemodilution rather than renal function directly. It is an indirect acuity marker with limited mechanistic linkage to AKI, though the 13–24h window may weakly track evolving illness.
Whether lymphocytes were measured mainly reflects lab-ordering/monitoring intensity in the immediate postop period. This is a care-process proxy with minimal direct renal specificity.
Measurement frequency is primarily a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy rather than a kidney injury signal. Even in 13–24h, it is not a physiologic marker of renal perfusion or tubular injury.

Having lymphocytes checked in the 25–36h window may indicate ongoing concern for complications/sepsis that can contribute to AKI. Still, it is a monitoring-intensity proxy and not a direct renal biomarker.
Glucose measurement (yes/no) is largely routine ICU monitoring and reflects practice patterns more than renal pathophysiology. Early postop timing further increases nonspecificity.

Whether glucose was checked is mostly a care-process signal (insulin protocol/diabetes monitoring) with weak renal specificity. It does not capture the magnitude of dysglycemia that might relate to AKI risk.

In the later window, continued glucose checks may correlate with persistent critical illness or insulin infusion, which can co-occur with AKI risk. However it remains a monitoring proxy, not a direct predictor of creatinine rise.
Ordering CK early is typically driven by concern for muscle injury/ischemia and is not kidney-specific. As a measured flag, it mainly reflects clinician suspicion rather than renal injury itself.

CK being checked may indicate rhabdomyolysis/ischemia concern, which can contribute to AKI, but the variable is only a measurement indicator. Thus it is an indirect proxy with limited predictive strength.

Late CK measurement may signal ongoing concern for muscle breakdown or limb ischemia, which can be relevant to AKI risk. Still, as a measured variable it is constrained by proxy penalty and cannot score high.

Mean eGFR in the first 12h reflects early postoperative renal function (derived from creatinine) and renal reserve. It is kidney-specific but may be confounded by immediate postop fluid shifts, so not maximal.
Mean eGFR at 13–24h captures the renal trajectory after initial resuscitation and is directly tied to evolving AKI. Mean aggregation reflects sustained dysfunction reasonably well in this phase.

Mean eGFR at 25–36h is a direct kidney function marker close to the Hour 60 outcome and strongly reflects evolving creatinine rise. This window provides high temporal proximity and strong prognostic linkage.

Maximum eGFR early likely reflects transient dilution/low creatinine rather than protection from AKI, making it less pathophysiologically informative. It is kidney-derived but the extreme in this direction is less useful than sustained/low values.

A higher eGFR extreme in 13–24h may reflect intermittent low creatinine from fluid balance rather than true renal recovery. It is renal-specific but less predictive than mean/low-function summaries for AKI progression.

Even close to outcome, the maximum eGFR tends to capture best-case moments and can be influenced by dilution, reducing its ability to predict worsening creatinine. It is renal-derived but redundant and weaker than the mean in this window.

Minimum eGFR in the immediate postop window reflects early renal function/creatinine rise, but can be transient from hemodilution and peri-CPB effects. Still a strong renal-specific signal, though slightly discounted for early timing.
Minimum eGFR during 13–24h captures evolving AKI vs recovery and is renal-specific. Trajectory-phase timing makes it a strong predictor of later creatinine ratio.

Minimum eGFR at 25–36h is a near-term direct marker of declining kidney function close to the Hour 60 outcome. The extreme (min) best captures clinically meaningful deterioration, making it a top-tier predictor.
Count/frequency of eGFR measurements mainly reflects monitoring intensity or clinician concern rather than physiology. Early postop labs are common, so this is a weak, indirect proxy.
Measurement frequency may increase with suspected AKI or instability, but remains largely a care-process proxy. Moderate proxy value only; not a direct renal mechanism.
More frequent eGFR checks late may signal evolving renal dysfunction, but it is still primarily clinician behavior/acuity proxy. Cannot score high per measured-variable rule.
LDH measurement frequency is a nonspecific monitoring marker (hemolysis/tissue injury workup) with weak direct linkage to AKI prediction. Mostly reflects practice patterns early after surgery.
LDH check frequency remains a nonspecific care-process signal and is not kidney-specific. Limited incremental value for predicting creatinine ratio.
Late LDH measurement frequency may reflect ongoing complications but is still nonspecific and behavior-driven. Minimal renal mechanistic relevance.

Mean INR early postop reflects coagulopathy/CPB effects and overall physiologic stress, which can correlate with AKI risk indirectly. Mean aggregation and early timing dilute short hypotension/perfusion insults, limiting renal specificity.

Persistently elevated mean INR at 25–36h may indicate hepatic dysfunction, bleeding, or severity of illness that can accompany AKI, but it is indirect. Mean INR is not a strong mechanistic renal predictor.
Peak INR in the first 12h captures worst coagulopathy/CPB effect and may proxy severity, transfusion, or shock risk. Still indirect for renal injury and early transient abnormalities are common.

A high INR peak at 25–36h suggests ongoing coagulopathy/hepatic dysfunction or major illness, which is more prognostically meaningful closer to Hour 60. It remains indirect but can be a moderate predictor of subsequent renal worsening.
Minimum INR early is usually clinically uninformative (often near normal) and does not capture a renal injury pathway. Low renal specificity and poor prognostic proximity.
Minimum INR at 25–36h is generally not a meaningful marker of complications relevant to AKI. Provides little renal-specific or prognostic signal compared with max/mean.

INR measurement frequency is primarily a monitoring/treatment-process proxy (bleeding/anticoagulation management) rather than renal physiology. Early postop INR checks are routine, so predictive value for creatinine ratio is minimal.

Indicates how often coagulation (INR) was checked in the 13–24h window, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity/bleeding or anticoagulation management. It is an indirect severity/procedure proxy with weak mechanistic linkage to AKI, so predictive value for creatinine_ratio is limited.

Frequency of INR checks closer to the outcome window may correlate with ongoing instability or anticoagulation issues. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy rather than renal physiology, so it remains a weak predictor.
